title: Alnylam — PH — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: OXLUMO (Lumasiran) in Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1

Product: OXLUMO (lumasiran) Manufacturer: Alnylam Pharmaceuticals Mechanism: RNAi therapeutic targeting HAO1 (glycolate oxidase), subcutaneous Indication: Treatment of PH1 to lower urinary oxalate (UOx) and plasma oxalate (POx) in pediatric and adult patients of all ages and all kidney stages Document scope: Product-level disease landscape, competitive dynamics, and commercial positioning Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscape

1.1 Epidemiology

Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1 (PH1):

  • Ultra-rare autosomal recessive metabolic disorder, the most severe form of primary hyperoxaluria
  • Prevalence: 1-3 cases per million individuals (historically); more recent 2025 genetic prevalence data suggests ~1:209,357 (~5 per million), indicating significant underdiagnosis
  • PH1 accounts for ~80% of all primary hyperoxaluria cases
  • Median age at symptom onset: ~5 years, but can present at any age from infancy to adulthood
  • A significant proportion of patients are diagnosed in adulthood, implying important underdetection
  • Estimated ~3,000-5,000 patients in the US; many more likely undiagnosed
  • Equal prevalence in males and females
  • Higher prevalence in consanguineous populations (North Africa, Middle East)

1.2 Disease Biology

PH1 is caused by mutations in the AGXT gene encoding alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase (AGT), a liver-specific peroxisomal enzyme. Key biology:

  • AGT deficiency: AGT normally converts glyoxylate to glycine in liver peroxisomes. When deficient, glyoxylate is instead oxidized to oxalate by glycolate oxidase (GO) or reduced to glycolate by glyoxylate reductase/hydroxypyruvate reductase (GRHPR)
  • Oxalate overproduction: The liver continuously overproduces oxalate, a toxic metabolite that cannot be metabolized in humans and must be excreted renally
  • Calcium oxalate crystal deposition: Excess oxalate combines with calcium to form insoluble calcium oxalate (CaOx) crystals that deposit in kidney tissue
  • Disease cascade: Crystal deposition causes nephrolithiasis (kidney stones), nephrocalcinosis, progressive chronic kidney disease, and ultimately end-stage kidney disease (ESKD)
  • Systemic oxalosis: Once renal clearance declines below the oxalate excretion threshold, systemic oxalate accumulation occurs with crystal deposition in bone, heart, retina, skin, and vasculature — a devastating multi-organ complication
  • Genotype-phenotype variability: Patients with identical AGXT genotypes, even within the same family, can have variable disease presentations — making prognosis unpredictable

1.3 The Oxalate Synthesis Pathway — Where OXLUMO and Rivfloza Act

Glycolate
    |
    v  (Glycolate Oxidase / HAO1) ← OXLUMO targets here (silences HAO1 mRNA)
    |
Glyoxylate
    |
    ├── AGT (deficient in PH1) → Glycine (normal pathway, broken)
    |
    ├── GRHPR → Glycolate (secondary pathway)
    |
    v  (Lactate Dehydrogenase / LDHA) ← Rivfloza targets here (silences LDHA mRNA)
    |
OXALATE (toxic, overproduced in PH1)
    |
    v
Calcium Oxalate Crystals → Kidney Stones → Nephrocalcinosis → CKD → ESKD → Systemic Oxalosis

Key distinction: OXLUMO acts upstream (blocking glycolate oxidase, reducing glyoxylate available for conversion to oxalate), while Rivfloza acts downstream (blocking lactate dehydrogenase, the final step of oxalate synthesis). Both use RNAi to silence their respective targets in hepatocytes.

1.4 Patient Journey and Diagnosis

Symptom Presentation:

  • Recurrent kidney stones (hallmark symptom; present in >80% of patients at diagnosis)
  • Early-onset kidney stones in children or adolescents — any kidney stone in a child should raise PH1 suspicion
  • Nephrocalcinosis (calcium oxalate deposits within kidney tissue)
  • Hematuria, UTIs, abdominal/flank pain
  • Failure to thrive in infants (infantile oxalosis — the most severe form)
  • Progressive renal function decline, sometimes sudden acute kidney decline in previously stable patients
  • Not all PH1 patients are stone formers — a critical but underappreciated fact

Diagnostic Pathway:

  1. Clinical suspicion triggered by recurrent kidney stones, especially in young patients, or family history
  2. Urine oxalate testing (24-hour UOx or spot UOx:creatinine ratio) — elevated UOx >75 mg/day (0.85 mmol/1.73m2/day) is suggestive
  3. Plasma oxalate levels — particularly important in patients with advanced CKD where urine testing is unreliable
  4. Stone analysis — 95%+ calcium oxalate composition is suggestive
  5. Renal imaging — nephrocalcinosis pattern
  6. Genetic testing for AGXT gene mutations — confirmatory gold standard; AUA recommends genetic testing in any patient with UOx >75 mg/day
  7. Metabolic evaluation — rule out secondary causes of hyperoxaluria

Diagnostic Challenges:

  • PH1 is frequently misdiagnosed or diagnosed late — non-specific symptoms overlap with common kidney stone disease
  • Median diagnostic delay can be years to decades
  • Urologists are primary stone managers but less familiar with PH1 treatment options beyond supportive care and transplant
  • Nephrologists are the primary decision makers for PH1 treatment, involved in diagnosis, treatment decisions, and ongoing management
  • Genetic testing adoption is growing but still not universally performed in stone-forming patients
  • ZoomRx ATU data shows: Nephrologists have higher rates of genetic testing; Urologists focus more on stone analysis and renal imaging

Specialty Dynamics (from ZoomRx ATU Wave 1, Dec 2024):

  • Nephrologists: Primary caregivers for PH1; involved in symptom management, diagnosis, and treatment decisions
  • Urologists: Involved in stone management but less likely to be primary treatment decision makers; less familiar with siRNA treatment options
  • Average HCPs in ZoomRx studies manage ~3 PH1 patients currently (62 total patients under active care across the sample)

1.5 Treatment Landscape

Pre-siRNA Era:

  • Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine): First-line supportive therapy; works in ~30% of PH1 patients with B6-responsive genotypes; reduces oxalate production by enhancing residual AGT activity
  • Supportive care: Hyperhydration, potassium citrate (crystallization inhibitor), low-oxalate diet — all reduce stone risk but do not address underlying oxalate overproduction
  • Dialysis: Required when kidney function declines to ESKD; intensive hemodialysis (5+ sessions/week, >3 hours each) needed to manage oxalate load
  • Organ transplantation: Combined liver-kidney transplantation (CLKT) or sequential liver-kidney transplantation (SLKT) — the only curative option pre-siRNA. Liver transplant corrects the metabolic defect; kidney transplant addresses renal damage. Significant morbidity, mortality risk, and organ availability constraints

Current Standard of Care:

Diagnosis of PH1 (AGXT mutation confirmed)
    |
    ├── Vitamin B6 trial (all patients; continue if responsive)
    |
    ├── Supportive care: Hyperhydration + Potassium citrate
    |
    ├── siRNA therapy initiation:
    |     ├── OXLUMO (lumasiran) — All ages, all kidney stages (including dialysis)
    |     └── Rivfloza (nedosiran) — Age ≥2, eGFR ≥30 only
    |
    ├── Combination: siRNA + B6 is common (56% of adult patients per ATU data)
    |
    └── ESKD pathway:
          ├── Intensive dialysis + siRNA
          └── Transplant (CLKT/SLKT) — increasingly deferred with siRNA availability

Treatment Sequencing (from ZoomRx ATU):

  • Most patients start on Vitamin B6 before transitioning to siRNA therapy
  • 56% of adult patients managed by nephrologists had OXLUMO added as adjuvant to Vitamin B6
  • Among urologist-treated adults previously on B6, 53% were switched to OXLUMO
  • OXLUMO is the dominant siRNA choice across both pediatric and adult patient populations
  • Use of OXLUMO + Rivfloza combination reported only among patients without transplant (small numbers)

1.6 Transplant Burden

PH1 carries one of the heaviest transplant burdens in rare disease:

  • Combined liver-kidney transplantation (CLKT) is the traditional curative approach — replaces the defective liver (corrects metabolic defect) and damaged kidneys
  • Sequential liver-kidney transplantation (SLKT) with intensive hemodialysis between transplants is an alternative
  • Pediatric patients with infantile oxalosis often require transplant before age 5
  • 5-year patient survival post-transplant: ~85%; graft survival: ~97% in recent series
  • siRNA therapies are transforming the transplant calculus — some patients who would have been listed for transplant are now managed medically with siRNA + dialysis
  • ZoomRx ATU data shows OXLUMO is preferred even for ESKD patients: 40% of nephrologists chose OXLUMO + dialysis (+/- B6) for a hypothetical ESKD patient profile

1.7 Unmet Needs

  1. Patient identification and early diagnosis: PH1 remains dramatically underdiagnosed; median diagnostic delay is years; improved screening of recurrent stone formers is needed
  2. Cost and access: High out-of-pocket costs and insurance coverage issues are the #1 barrier to prescribing (per both ATU and PET data); even current users cite OOP costs
  3. Treatment in advanced kidney disease: Rivfloza is restricted to eGFR ≥30; OXLUMO covers all kidney stages including dialysis — but outcomes in advanced disease are less studied
  4. Transplant avoidance validation: Long-term data on whether siRNA therapy can definitively prevent or defer transplant is still accumulating
  5. Disease awareness among urologists: Urologists manage most kidney stone patients but are less familiar with PH1-specific therapies and less likely to consider genetic testing
  6. Oral treatment options: Both siRNA therapies require subcutaneous injection; an oral therapy remains an unmet need, particularly for lifelong treatment
  7. Genotype-specific treatment optimization: Variable disease progression even within families makes treatment decision-making challenging

2. Product Profiles

2.1 OXLUMO (lumasiran) — Alnylam Pharmaceuticals

Mechanism of Action / Delivery: Lumasiran is an RNAi therapeutic (small interfering RNA) that targets HAO1 mRNA encoding glycolate oxidase (GO) in hepatocytes. By silencing HAO1 and depleting the GO enzyme, OXLUMO reduces conversion of glycolate to glyoxylate, thereby reducing the substrate available for oxalate production. Lumasiran is delivered to hepatocytes via GalNAc (N-acetylgalactosamine) conjugation, which binds to the asialoglycoprotein receptor on hepatocyte surfaces for efficient liver-specific uptake. Administered subcutaneously.

Approved Indication: Treatment of PH1 to lower urinary oxalate (UOx) and plasma oxalate (POx) in pediatric and adult patients of all ages and all stages of kidney function (including patients on dialysis).

Regulatory History:

  • November 2020: First global approvals (EU and US) for PH1 in all age groups
  • Subsequently received supplemental approval for advanced PH1 (including patients with advanced kidney disease and on hemodialysis)

Key Clinical Data — ILLUMINATE Trial Program:

  • ILLUMINATE-A (Phase 3, ≥6 years, randomized, placebo-controlled):

    • Primary endpoint met: Significant reduction in 24-hour UOx excretion vs placebo
    • 53% reduction in 24-hour UOx from baseline sustained through month 36
    • 84% of OXLUMO patients achieved normal (52%) or near-normal (32%) oxalate levels
    • eGFR remained stable through 36 months of treatment
    • Kidney stone event rate reduced from 3.19/patient-year (pre-treatment) to 0.47-0.54/patient-year during treatment
    • 50-62% of patients had zero kidney stone events during lumasiran treatment

  • ILLUMINATE-B (Phase 3, <6 years, open-label):

    • 72% reduction in spot UOx:Cr from baseline sustained through month 30
    • eGFR stable; safety consistent with ILLUMINATE-A

  • ILLUMINATE-C (Phase 3, advanced CKD / dialysis):

    • Significant reduction in plasma oxalate (POx) from baseline at 6 months in patients with eGFR <30 and on hemodialysis
    • Demonstrated activity in the most severe patient population

  • Long-term data (through 42 months):

    • UOx reductions >60% sustained from month 4 to month 42
    • Kidney stone event rates remained reduced
    • eGFR remained stable throughout study duration
    • POx reductions of 35-36% sustained

Safety Profile:

  • Most common adverse reaction: injection site reactions (erythema, swelling, pain, hematoma, pruritus, discoloration) — generally mild, resolving within 1 day
  • No significant safety signals in long-term extension data
  • Well tolerated across all age groups and kidney function stages

Dosing:

  • Loading dose: 3 mg/kg SC once monthly for 3 doses (patients ≥10 kg)
  • Maintenance: 3 mg/kg SC once quarterly (every 3 months)
  • Weight-based dosing for patients <10 kg
  • HCP-administered; some patients may qualify for at-home injection depending on insurance

Commercial Position:

  • First-mover advantage: Approved November 2020, ~3 years before Rivfloza (September 2023)
  • Only PH1 treatment approved for ALL ages and ALL kidney stages (including dialysis)
  • Quarterly dosing (vs Rivfloza's monthly dosing)
  • Largest PH1 clinical trial program (ILLUMINATE A, B, C)
  • Alnylam Assist patient support: 94% of patients have no out-of-pocket costs; 95% of payers cover OXLUMO nationally
  • Value-based agreement framework with Patient Need Adjustment (PNA) component for payers

Revenue:

  • FY2024: ~$167M global net product revenues (+29% YoY)
  • FY2025: Combined GIVLAARI + OXLUMO ~$109M/quarter (Q2 2025); ~20% YoY patient growth
  • Part of Alnylam's rare disease franchise alongside GIVLAARI (for AHP) and AMVUTTRA/ONPATTRO (for hATTR)
  • Alnylam 2030 strategy positions rare disease franchise for continued growth

2.2 Rivfloza (nedosiran) — Novo Nordisk (Dicerna)

Mechanism of Action / Delivery: Nedosiran is an RNAi therapeutic (siRNA) targeting LDHA mRNA encoding hepatic lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). By silencing LDHA, Rivfloza blocks the final step in oxalate synthesis, reducing conversion of glyoxylate to oxalate. Also uses GalNAc conjugation for hepatocyte-specific delivery. Administered subcutaneously.

Approved Indication: Lowering urinary oxalate levels in children age ≥2 years and adults with PH1 and relatively preserved kidney function (eGFR ≥30 mL/min/1.73m2).

Key Limitation vs OXLUMO: Restricted to patients with eGFR ≥30 — cannot be used in advanced CKD or dialysis patients.

Regulatory History:

  • September 2023: FDA approval for patients ≥9 years with eGFR ≥30
  • March 2025: Label expansion to children ≥2 years

Key Clinical Data:

  • PHYOX2 (Phase 3, pivotal):

    • Primary endpoint met: Significant reduction in 24-hour UOx from Day 90 to Day 180 vs placebo
    • Reduced UOx to normal or near-normal levels in the majority of patients

  • PHYOX8 (Pediatric):

    • 64% reduction in spot UOx:Cr ratio from baseline at Month 6 in pediatric patients

  • PHYOX3 (Long-term extension):

    • Long-term safety and efficacy data; UOx reductions sustained

Dosing:

  • Monthly subcutaneous injection (80mg, 128mg, or 160mg based on weight)
  • Self- or caregiver-administered at home (for patients ≥12 years) — key competitive differentiator

Competitive Differentiators vs OXLUMO:

  • Self-administration at home by patient or caregiver — reduces HCP visits
  • Monthly dosing (vs OXLUMO quarterly)
  • Targets the "final step" in oxalate synthesis (LDHA) — a distinct MOA narrative
  • Perception of better affordability/access among HCPs (even among those without Rivfloza experience — per ATU data)
  • Newer product — novelty drives favorable change in product perception post-rep interaction

Competitive Disadvantages vs OXLUMO:

  • eGFR ≥30 restriction excludes advanced CKD and dialysis patients
  • Shorter track record (approved 2023 vs 2020)
  • Smaller clinical trial program
  • Lower HCP awareness and familiarity (per ATU and PET data)
  • OXLUMO has broader label: ALL ages, ALL kidney stages

2.3 Supportive Therapies

Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine):

  • First-line supportive therapy; effective in ~30% of PH1 patients with B6-responsive AGXT mutations
  • Reduces oxalate production by enhancing residual AGT enzyme activity
  • Most commonly used in combination with siRNA therapy (56% of adult patients on siRNA were previously on B6)
  • Inexpensive, oral, well-tolerated

Potassium Citrate / Crystallization Inhibitors:

  • Reduce calcium oxalate crystal formation by increasing urinary citrate
  • Adjunctive, symptom-directed; do not address oxalate overproduction

Hyperhydration:

  • Dilutes urine oxalate concentration; reduces stone formation risk
  • Requires significant lifestyle burden (high fluid intake maintained throughout the day)

Transplantation:

  • CLKT (combined liver-kidney) or SLKT (sequential liver-kidney) — curative but high-risk
  • Increasingly deferred in the siRNA era; some patients managed long-term with siRNA + dialysis instead of transplant

3. Competitive Dynamics

3.1 OXLUMO vs Rivfloza — The Central Competitive Axis

This is the defining competitive dynamic in PH1 — two RNAi therapies from different companies targeting different enzymes in the same pathway. Key battleground dimensions:

Dimension OXLUMO (Alnylam) Rivfloza (Novo Nordisk)
Target HAO1 / Glycolate oxidase (upstream) LDHA / Lactate dehydrogenase (downstream)
Approval Nov 2020 (first-mover) Sep 2023
Age range All ages ≥2 years (expanded Mar 2025)
Kidney function All stages including dialysis eGFR ≥30 only
Dosing Quarterly SC (after loading) Monthly SC
Administration HCP-administered Self/caregiver-administered (≥12y)
Trial program ILLUMINATE A, B, C (largest in PH1) PHYOX 1, 2, 3, 8
UOx reduction 53% (ILLUMINATE-A, 36mo) Significant (PHYOX2, 6mo)
eGFR stability Demonstrated through 36+ months Demonstrated
Patient support Alnylam Assist (94% $0 OOP) Novo patient support

3.2 HCP Perceptions (from ZoomRx PET Q3 2025 and ATU Wave 1)

Brand Awareness and Preference:

  • OXLUMO is the most top-of-mind PH1 therapy recalled by both nephrologists and urologists (unaided)
  • Aided awareness: OXLUMO 97%/95% (Neph/Uro) vs Rivfloza 86%/70% — OXLUMO leads significantly
  • OXLUMO has strong first-mover advantage with HCPs being well-informed about its MOA and FDA approval
  • Rivfloza's entry does NOT currently pose a significant threat to OXLUMO's adoption

Product Perceptions:

  • Nephrologists rate OXLUMO higher on efficacy in lowering oxalate levels and MOA
  • Nephrologists rate Rivfloza higher on dosing frequency and out-of-pocket cost
  • Better Rivfloza affordability/access perceptions exist even among HCPs WITHOUT Rivfloza experience — suggesting a marketing perception gap vs reality
  • OXLUMO users have higher product perceptions than non-users across most attributes (non-users drive lower OOP cost and mode-of-administration scores)

Prescribing Drivers:

  • Top clinical driver: Efficacy in lowering urinary/plasma oxalate levels (both products)
  • Nephrologists: Efficacy in lowering oxalate is #1 clinical driver for OXLUMO
  • Urologists: Change in kidney stone events is #1 clinical driver for OXLUMO
  • Top non-clinical driver: Quality of life improvements (OXLUMO); access/formulary considerations more prominent for Rivfloza
  • Key barriers for OXLUMO: High OOP costs, insurance coverage issues — even among current users
  • Key barriers for Rivfloza: Limited time on market, unfamiliarity with clinical data

Future Usage Expectations (ATU):

  • OXLUMO future usage among pediatric patients expected to remain consistent
  • Rivfloza not expected to increase in pediatric population
  • Among adult patients, treatment share expected to remain consistent in next 12 months
  • Majority of HCPs do NOT intend to increase Rivfloza usage; some cite OXLUMO's unproven efficacy in their current patients and patient preference for self-injection as reasons for not increasing OXLUMO

3.3 Promotional Effectiveness (from ZoomRx PET Data)

Share of Voice and Reach:

  • OXLUMO leads Rivfloza in share of voice, driven by higher HCP reach and frequency of rep interactions
  • OXLUMO reach: 47-51% of HCPs; Rivfloza reach: lower across all segments
  • On-list HCPs have higher reach for both products; off-list HCPs have lower reach

Message Performance — OXLUMO:

  • OX1 ("only approved quarterly SC injection for PH1, all ages, all kidney stages"): Most recalled (66%) AND most effective (ME 70%)
  • OX5 ("ILLUMINATE A and B: eGFR stable at 30-36 months"): High recall (62%), good effectiveness (ME 65%)
  • OX2 ("tested in severely affected, advanced kidney disease, dialysis patients"): Low recall (38%) but HIGH effectiveness (ME 69%) — opportunity to amplify
  • OX3 ("largest PH1 trial program"): Low recall (38%) but high effectiveness (ME 66%) — another underleveraged message
  • OX8 ("Alnylam Assist, 94% pay nothing, 95% payer coverage"): Patient support message with solid recall
  • Average OXLUMO message recall: 50%; average ME: 65% — both above rare disease industry averages

Message Performance — Rivfloza:

  • RI2 ("approved for PH1 age ≥2, now prescribable"): Most recalled
  • RI9 ("only self/caregiver-administered PH1 treatment"): Most effective message across BOTH brands (ME up to 90% in interim data)
  • RI3 ("significantly reduced UOx in most PH1 patients — PHYOX2"): High effectiveness
  • Overall: OXLUMO messages rated MORE effective on average than Rivfloza messages; however, Rivfloza's self-administration message (RI9) is the single most effective message across brands

Sales Rep Effectiveness:

  • OXLUMO reps rate at parity or better than Rivfloza reps across all rep attributes
  • OXLUMO reps more likely to get "recommend to colleagues" and "compelling reason to Rx"
  • Rivfloza reps rate higher in favorably changing product perception — driven by novelty
  • Key OXLUMO opportunity: Rep preparation/organization (slightly below industry average — correlated with overall call quality)

Longitudinal Trends (Q4 2023 Baseline → Q3 2025):

  • OXLUMO reach declined from 81% (Q4 2023) to 47% (Q3 2025) — significant drop; Rivfloza entry split the share of voice
  • OXLUMO overall sales call quality remained high: 79% (Q4 2023) → 83% (Q3 2025)
  • Likelihood to increase Rx improved: 59% (Q4 2023) → 66% (Q3 2025)
  • Message recall average stable: 44% (Q4 2023) → 50% (Q3 2025) — improvement
  • Net Promoter Score improved from 10 (Q4 2023) to 88% recommend to colleagues (Q3 2025)
  • Rivfloza interim data shows reps catching up on key metrics — Rivfloza reps more likely to positively impact likelihood to prescribe and product perception

3.4 MSL and Non-Personal Promotion

  • MSL impact is similar for OXLUMO and Rivfloza; HCPs agree MSLs impacted understanding of appropriate product use more than clinical understanding of PH1
  • Over half of HCPs received emails from Alnylam sales reps in past 90 days (directionally greater than Novo Nordisk)
  • Efficacy is the most aided/unaided recalled topic for both manufacturers' digital communications
  • Preferred information sources: online medical journals/magazines (#1), emails from sales reps (#2)
  • Community HCPs rely more on sales rep interactions; academic HCPs rely more on journals/publications

4. Market Access and Pricing

4.1 OXLUMO Pricing and Access

  • OXLUMO WAC (wholesale acquisition cost): ~$493,000/year (approximate; weight-based dosing varies)
  • Ultra-rare orphan drug pricing model
  • Alnylam Assist patient support program:
    • 94% of OXLUMO patients have $0 out-of-pocket costs
    • 95% of payers offer national coverage
    • Commercial copay program for commercially insured patients
    • Patient Assistance Program (PAP) for uninsured/underinsured
    • Dedicated case managers assigned within 2 business days
  • Value-based agreement framework with Patient Need Adjustment (PNA) component — provides payers predictability on per-patient costs
  • High OOP costs and insurance coverage remain the #1 prescribing barrier — even among OXLUMO users; barrier is MORE pronounced among non-users and urologists

4.2 Market Access Dynamics

  • Insurance coverage issues and high OOP costs cited as top barrier by both nephrologists and urologists
  • Non-users perceive OOP costs as a greater barrier than users — suggesting once access is secured, perception improves
  • Rivfloza benefits from a perception of better affordability/access even among HCPs without direct experience — Alnylam has an opportunity to counter this with real-world access data
  • Access/formulary considerations are a more prominent prescribing driver for Rivfloza than OXLUMO
  • Formulary access/reimbursement sheets used more often in Rivfloza high-impact interactions — suggesting Novo Nordisk is leaning into the access narrative

5. Key Implications for ZoomRx Engagement

5.1 Study Portfolio

ZoomRx currently runs two primary study types for OXLUMO:

  1. Promotional Effectiveness Tracker (PET):

    • Tracks OXLUMO vs Rivfloza rep interactions, messaging, sales rep effectiveness
    • Ongoing since Q4 2023 (baseline wave); latest wave Q3 2025
    • Head-to-head: HCPs can report interactions for either or both products
    • 30 unique physicians per wave; Nephrology + Urology
    • Industry average benchmarked against rare disease indications

  2. Awareness, Trial, and Usage (ATU):

    • Full market landscape study: awareness, treatment patterns, switching behavior, product perceptions, barriers
    • Wave 1 fielded Nov-Dec 2024; 60 HCPs
    • Covers hypothetical patient profiles to understand treatment decision-making
    • Includes rep interaction recall and information source preferences

5.2 Alnylam Commercial Context

  • Alnylam Pharmaceuticals: Pioneer of RNAi therapeutics; first FDA-approved RNAi drug (ONPATTRO, 2018)
  • Rare disease portfolio: ONPATTRO/AMVUTTRA (hATTR), GIVLAARI (AHP), OXLUMO (PH1)
  • Combined 2026 guidance: $5,300M net product revenue (~71% growth vs 2025)
  • "Alnylam 2030" strategy launched January 2026 — positions next era of growth
  • Same Alnylam rep may detail OXLUMO and GIVLAARI (combined PET readout covers both)
  • Primary contact for OXLUMO studies: sneha.shah@zoomrx.com

5.3 Strategic Watch Items

  1. Rivfloza label expansion trajectory: Novo Nordisk may pursue expanded kidney function labels; any relaxation of the eGFR ≥30 restriction would narrow OXLUMO's exclusive advantage in advanced CKD
  2. Self-administration perception: Rivfloza's self-administration message (RI9) is the most effective message across BOTH brands — Alnylam needs to address the administration convenience gap (at-home HCP injection for OXLUMO vs true self-administration for Rivfloza)
  3. Access narrative war: Rivfloza perceived as more affordable even without experience — Alnylam needs aggressive real-world access data amplification
  4. Urologist education gap: Urologists less familiar with siRNA options, less likely to suspect PH1, less likely to order genetic testing — a patient identification opportunity
  5. Pediatric stronghold: OXLUMO is dominant in pediatric PH1; Rivfloza's pediatric label expansion to age ≥2 (March 2025) may erode this, but OXLUMO's all-ages/all-stages position remains stronger
  6. Long-term transplant deferral data: As 5+ year data accumulates, evidence of transplant avoidance with siRNA therapy could transform the treatment paradigm and health economics narrative

headings.text:
1. Disease Landscape
1.1 Epidemiology
Primary Hyperoxaluria Type 1 (PH1):

Ultra-rare autosomal recessive metabolic disorder, the most severe form of primary hyperoxaluria
Prevalence: 1-3 cases per million individuals (historically); more recent 2025 genetic prevalence data suggests ~1:209,357 (~5 per million), indicating significant underdiagnosis
PH1 accounts for ~80% of all primary hyperoxaluria cases
Median age at symptom onset: ~5 years, but can present at any age from infancy to adulthood
A significant proportion of patients are diagnosed in adulthood, implying important underdetection
Estimated ~3,000-5,000 patients in the US; many more likely undiagnosed
Equal prevalence in males and females
Higher prevalence in consanguineous populations (North Africa, Middle East)

1.2 Disease Biology
PH1 is caused by mutations in the AGXT gene encoding alanine-glyoxylate aminotransferase (AGT), a liver-specific peroxisomal enzyme. Key biology:

AGT deficiency: AGT normally converts glyoxylate to glycine in liver peroxisomes. When deficient, glyoxylate is instead oxidized to oxalate by glycolate oxidase (GO) or reduced to glycolate by glyoxylate reductase/hydroxypyruvate reductase (GRHPR)
Oxalate overproduction: The liver continuously overproduces oxalate, a toxic metabolite that cannot be metabolized in humans and must be excreted renally
Calcium oxalate crystal deposition: Excess oxalate combines with calcium to form insoluble calcium oxalate (CaOx) crystals that deposit in kidney tissue
Disease cascade: Crystal deposition causes nephrolithiasis (kidney stones), nephrocalcinosis, progressive chronic kidney disease, and ultimately end-stage kidney disease (ESKD)
Systemic oxalosis: Once renal clearance declines below the oxalate excretion threshold, systemic oxalate accumulation occurs with crystal deposition in bone, heart, retina, skin, and vasculature — a devastating multi-organ complication
Genotype-phenotype variability: Patients with identical AGXT genotypes, even within the same family, can have variable disease presentations — making prognosis unpredictable

1.3 The Oxalate Synthesis Pathway — Where OXLUMO and Rivfloza Act
Glycolate
    |
    v  (Glycolate Oxidase / HAO1) ← OXLUMO targets here (silences HAO1 mRNA)
    |
Glyoxylate
    |
    ├── AGT (deficient in PH1) → Glycine (normal pathway, broken)
    |
    ├── GRHPR → Glycolate (secondary pathway)
    |
    v  (Lactate Dehydrogenase / LDHA) ← Rivfloza targets here (silences LDHA mRNA)
    |
OXALATE (toxic, overproduced in PH1)
    |
    v
Calcium Oxalate Crystals → Kidney Stones → Nephrocalcinosis → CKD → ESKD → Systemic Oxalosis

Key distinction: OXLUMO acts upstream (blocking glycolate oxidase, reducing glyoxylate available for conversion to oxalate), while Rivfloza acts downstream (blocking lactate dehydrogenase, the final step of oxalate synthesis). Both use RNAi to silence their respective targets in hepatocytes.
1.4 Patient Journey and Diagnosis
Symptom Presentation:

Recurrent kidney stones (hallmark symptom; present in >80% of patients at diagnosis)
Early-onset kidney stones in children or adolescents — any kidney stone in a child should raise PH1 suspicion
Nephrocalcinosis (calcium oxalate deposits within kidney tissue)
Hematuria, UTIs, abdominal/flank pain
Failure to thrive in infants (infantile oxalosis — the most severe form)
Progressive renal function decline, sometimes sudden acute kidney decline in previously stable patients
Not all PH1 patients are stone formers — a critical but underappreciated fact

Diagnostic Pathway:

Clinical suspicion triggered by recurrent kidney stones, especially in young patients, or family history
Urine oxalate testing (24-hour UOx or spot UOx:creatinine ratio) — elevated UOx >75 mg/day (0.85 mmol/1.73m2/day) is suggestive
Plasma oxalate levels — particularly important in patients with advanced CKD where urine testing is unreliable
Stone analysis — 95%+ calcium oxalate composition is suggestive
Renal imaging — nephrocalcinosis pattern
Genetic testing for AGXT gene mutations — confirmatory gold standard; AUA recommends genetic testing in any patient with UOx >75 mg/day
Metabolic evaluation — rule out secondary causes of hyperoxaluria

Diagnostic Challenges:

PH1 is frequently misdiagnosed or diagnosed late — non-specific symptoms overlap with common kidney stone disease
Median diagnostic delay can be years to decades
Urologists are primary stone managers but less familiar with PH1 treatment options beyond supportive care and transplant
Nephrologists are the primary decision makers for PH1 treatment, involved in diagnosis, treatment decisions, and ongoing management
Genetic testing adoption is growing but still not universally performed in stone-forming patients
ZoomRx ATU data shows: Nephrologists have higher rates of genetic testing; Urologists focus more on stone analysis and renal imaging

Specialty Dynamics (from ZoomRx ATU Wave 1, Dec 2024):

Nephrologists: Primary caregivers for PH1; involved in symptom management, diagnosis, and treatment decisions
Urologists: Involved in stone management but less likely to be primary treatment decision makers; less familiar with siRNA treatment options
Average HCPs in ZoomRx studies manage ~3 PH1 patients currently (62 total patients under active care across the sample)

1.5 Treatment Landscape
Pre-siRNA Era:

Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine): First-line supportive therapy; works in ~30% of PH1 patients with B6-responsive genotypes; reduces oxalate production by enhancing residual AGT activity
Supportive care: Hyperhydration, potassium citrate (crystallization inhibitor), low-oxalate diet — all reduce stone risk but do not address underlying oxalate overproduction
Dialysis: Required when kidney function declines to ESKD; intensive hemodialysis (5+ sessions/week, >3 hours each) needed to manage oxalate load
Organ transplantation: Combined liver-kidney transplantation (CLKT) or sequential liver-kidney transplantation (SLKT) — the only curative option pre-siRNA. Liver transplant corrects the metabolic defect; kidney transplant addresses renal damage. Significant morbidity, mortality risk, and organ availability constraints

Current Standard of Care:
Diagnosis of PH1 (AGXT mutation confirmed)
    |
    ├── Vitamin B6 trial (all patients; continue if responsive)
    |
    ├── Supportive care: Hyperhydration + Potassium citrate
    |
    ├── siRNA therapy initiation:
    |     ├── OXLUMO (lumasiran) — All ages, all kidney stages (including dialysis)
    |     └── Rivfloza (nedosiran) — Age ≥2, eGFR ≥30 only
    |
    ├── Combination: siRNA + B6 is common (56% of adult patients per ATU data)
    |
    └── ESKD pathway:
          ├── Intensive dialysis + siRNA
          └── Transplant (CLKT/SLKT) — increasingly deferred with siRNA availability

Treatment Sequencing (from ZoomRx ATU):

Most patients start on Vitamin B6 before transitioning to siRNA therapy
56% of adult patients managed by nephrologists had OXLUMO added as adjuvant to Vitamin B6
Among urologist-treated adults previously on B6, 53% were switched to OXLUMO
OXLUMO is the dominant siRNA choice across both pediatric and adult patient populations
Use of OXLUMO + Rivfloza combination reported only among patients without transplant (small numbers)

1.6 Transplant Burden
PH1 carries one of the heaviest transplant burdens in rare disease:

Combined liver-kidney transplantation (CLKT) is the traditional curative approach — replaces the defective liver (corrects metabolic defect) and damaged kidneys
Sequential liver-kidney transplantation (SLKT) with intensive hemodialysis between transplants is an alternative
Pediatric patients with infantile oxalosis often require transplant before age 5
5-year patient survival post-transplant: ~85%; graft survival: ~97% in recent series
siRNA therapies are transforming the transplant calculus — some patients who would have been listed for transplant are now managed medically with siRNA + dialysis
ZoomRx ATU data shows OXLUMO is preferred even for ESKD patients: 40% of nephrologists chose OXLUMO + dialysis (+/- B6) for a hypothetical ESKD patient profile

1.7 Unmet Needs

Patient identification and early diagnosis: PH1 remains dramatically underdiagnosed; median diagnostic delay is years; improved screening of recurrent stone formers is needed
Cost and access: High out-of-pocket costs and insurance coverage issues are the #1 barrier to prescribing (per both ATU and PET data); even current users cite OOP costs
Treatment in advanced kidney disease: Rivfloza is restricted to eGFR ≥30; OXLUMO covers all kidney stages including dialysis — but outcomes in advanced disease are less studied
Transplant avoidance validation: Long-term data on whether siRNA therapy can definitively prevent or defer transplant is still accumulating
Disease awareness among urologists: Urologists manage most kidney stone patients but are less familiar with PH1-specific therapies and less likely to consider genetic testing
Oral treatment options: Both siRNA therapies require subcutaneous injection; an oral therapy remains an unmet need, particularly for lifelong treatment
Genotype-specific treatment optimization: Variable disease progression even within families makes treatment decision-making challenging


2. Product Profiles
2.1 OXLUMO (lumasiran) — Alnylam Pharmaceuticals
Mechanism of Action / Delivery:
Lumasiran is an RNAi therapeutic (small interfering RNA) that targets HAO1 mRNA encoding glycolate oxidase (GO) in hepatocytes. By silencing HAO1 and depleting the GO enzyme, OXLUMO reduces conversion of glycolate to glyoxylate, thereby reducing the substrate available for oxalate production. Lumasiran is delivered to hepatocytes via GalNAc (N-acetylgalactosamine) conjugation, which binds to the asialoglycoprotein receptor on hepatocyte surfaces for efficient liver-specific uptake. Administered subcutaneously.
Approved Indication:
Treatment of PH1 to lower urinary oxalate (UOx) and plasma oxalate (POx) in pediatric and adult patients of all ages and all stages of kidney function (including patients on dialysis).
Regulatory History:

November 2020: First global approvals (EU and US) for PH1 in all age groups
Subsequently received supplemental approval for advanced PH1 (including patients with advanced kidney disease and on hemodialysis)

Key Clinical Data — ILLUMINATE Trial Program:


ILLUMINATE-A (Phase 3, ≥6 years, randomized, placebo-controlled):

Primary endpoint met: Significant reduction in 24-hour UOx excretion vs placebo
53% reduction in 24-hour UOx from baseline sustained through month 36
84% of OXLUMO patients achieved normal (52%) or near-normal (32%) oxalate levels
eGFR remained stable through 36 months of treatment
Kidney stone event rate reduced from 3.19/patient-year (pre-treatment) to 0.47-0.54/patient-year during treatment
50-62% of patients had zero kidney stone events during lumasiran treatment



ILLUMINATE-B (Phase 3, <6 years, open-label):

72% reduction in spot UOx:Cr from baseline sustained through month 30
eGFR stable; safety consistent with ILLUMINATE-A



ILLUMINATE-C (Phase 3, advanced CKD / dialysis):

Significant reduction in plasma oxalate (POx) from baseline at 6 months in patients with eGFR <30 and on hemodialysis
Demonstrated activity in the most severe patient population



Long-term data (through 42 months):

UOx reductions >60% sustained from month 4 to month 42
Kidney stone event rates remained reduced
eGFR remained stable throughout study duration
POx reductions of 35-36% sustained



Safety Profile:

Most common adverse reaction: injection site reactions (erythema, swelling, pain, hematoma, pruritus, discoloration) — generally mild, resolving within 1 day
No significant safety signals in long-term extension data
Well tolerated across all age groups and kidney function stages

Dosing:

Loading dose: 3 mg/kg SC once monthly for 3 doses (patients ≥10 kg)
Maintenance: 3 mg/kg SC once quarterly (every 3 months)
Weight-based dosing for patients <10 kg
HCP-administered; some patients may qualify for at-home injection depending on insurance

Commercial Position:

First-mover advantage: Approved November 2020, ~3 years before Rivfloza (September 2023)
Only PH1 treatment approved for ALL ages and ALL kidney stages (including dialysis)
Quarterly dosing (vs Rivfloza's monthly dosing)
Largest PH1 clinical trial program (ILLUMINATE A, B, C)
Alnylam Assist patient support: 94% of patients have no out-of-pocket costs; 95% of payers cover OXLUMO nationally
Value-based agreement framework with Patient Need Adjustment (PNA) component for payers

Revenue:

FY2024: ~$167M global net product revenues (+29% YoY)
FY2025: Combined GIVLAARI + OXLUMO ~$109M/quarter (Q2 2025); ~20% YoY patient growth
Part of Alnylam's rare disease franchise alongside GIVLAARI (for AHP) and AMVUTTRA/ONPATTRO (for hATTR)
Alnylam 2030 strategy positions rare disease franchise for continued growth

2.2 Rivfloza (nedosiran) — Novo Nordisk (Dicerna)
Mechanism of Action / Delivery:
Nedosiran is an RNAi therapeutic (siRNA) targeting LDHA mRNA encoding hepatic lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). By silencing LDHA, Rivfloza blocks the final step in oxalate synthesis, reducing conversion of glyoxylate to oxalate. Also uses GalNAc conjugation for hepatocyte-specific delivery. Administered subcutaneously.
Approved Indication:
Lowering urinary oxalate levels in children age ≥2 years and adults with PH1 and relatively preserved kidney function (eGFR ≥30 mL/min/1.73m2).
Key Limitation vs OXLUMO: Restricted to patients with eGFR ≥30 — cannot be used in advanced CKD or dialysis patients.
Regulatory History:

September 2023: FDA approval for patients ≥9 years with eGFR ≥30
March 2025: Label expansion to children ≥2 years

Key Clinical Data:


PHYOX2 (Phase 3, pivotal):

Primary endpoint met: Significant reduction in 24-hour UOx from Day 90 to Day 180 vs placebo
Reduced UOx to normal or near-normal levels in the majority of patients



PHYOX8 (Pediatric):

64% reduction in spot UOx:Cr ratio from baseline at Month 6 in pediatric patients



PHYOX3 (Long-term extension):

Long-term safety and efficacy data; UOx reductions sustained



Dosing:

Monthly subcutaneous injection (80mg, 128mg, or 160mg based on weight)
Self- or caregiver-administered at home (for patients ≥12 years) — key competitive differentiator

Competitive Differentiators vs OXLUMO:

Self-administration at home by patient or caregiver — reduces HCP visits
Monthly dosing (vs OXLUMO quarterly)
Targets the "final step" in oxalate synthesis (LDHA) — a distinct MOA narrative
Perception of better affordability/access among HCPs (even among those without Rivfloza experience — per ATU data)
Newer product — novelty drives favorable change in product perception post-rep interaction

Competitive Disadvantages vs OXLUMO:

eGFR ≥30 restriction excludes advanced CKD and dialysis patients
Shorter track record (approved 2023 vs 2020)
Smaller clinical trial program
Lower HCP awareness and familiarity (per ATU and PET data)
OXLUMO has broader label: ALL ages, ALL kidney stages

2.3 Supportive Therapies
Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine):

First-line supportive therapy; effective in ~30% of PH1 patients with B6-responsive AGXT mutations
Reduces oxalate production by enhancing residual AGT enzyme activity
Most commonly used in combination with siRNA therapy (56% of adult patients on siRNA were previously on B6)
Inexpensive, oral, well-tolerated

Potassium Citrate / Crystallization Inhibitors:

Reduce calcium oxalate crystal formation by increasing urinary citrate
Adjunctive, symptom-directed; do not address oxalate overproduction

Hyperhydration:

Dilutes urine oxalate concentration; reduces stone formation risk
Requires significant lifestyle burden (high fluid intake maintained throughout the day)

Transplantation:

CLKT (combined liver-kidney) or SLKT (sequential liver-kidney) — curative but high-risk
Increasingly deferred in the siRNA era; some patients managed long-term with siRNA + dialysis instead of transplant

3.2 HCP Perceptions (from ZoomRx PET Q3 2025 and ATU Wave 1)
Brand Awareness and Preference:

OXLUMO is the most top-of-mind PH1 therapy recalled by both nephrologists and urologists (unaided)
Aided awareness: OXLUMO 97%/95% (Neph/Uro) vs Rivfloza 86%/70% — OXLUMO leads significantly
OXLUMO has strong first-mover advantage with HCPs being well-informed about its MOA and FDA approval
Rivfloza's entry does NOT currently pose a significant threat to OXLUMO's adoption

Product Perceptions:

Nephrologists rate OXLUMO higher on efficacy in lowering oxalate levels and MOA
Nephrologists rate Rivfloza higher on dosing frequency and out-of-pocket cost
Better Rivfloza affordability/access perceptions exist even among HCPs WITHOUT Rivfloza experience — suggesting a marketing perception gap vs reality
OXLUMO users have higher product perceptions than non-users across most attributes (non-users drive lower OOP cost and mode-of-administration scores)

Prescribing Drivers:

Top clinical driver: Efficacy in lowering urinary/plasma oxalate levels (both products)
Nephrologists: Efficacy in lowering oxalate is #1 clinical driver for OXLUMO
Urologists: Change in kidney stone events is #1 clinical driver for OXLUMO
Top non-clinical driver: Quality of life improvements (OXLUMO); access/formulary considerations more prominent for Rivfloza
Key barriers for OXLUMO: High OOP costs, insurance coverage issues — even among current users
Key barriers for Rivfloza: Limited time on market, unfamiliarity with clinical data

Future Usage Expectations (ATU):

OXLUMO future usage among pediatric patients expected to remain consistent
Rivfloza not expected to increase in pediatric population
Among adult patients, treatment share expected to remain consistent in next 12 months
Majority of HCPs do NOT intend to increase Rivfloza usage; some cite OXLUMO's unproven efficacy in their current patients and patient preference for self-injection as reasons for not increasing OXLUMO

3.3 Promotional Effectiveness (from ZoomRx PET Data)
Share of Voice and Reach:

OXLUMO leads Rivfloza in share of voice, driven by higher HCP reach and frequency of rep interactions
OXLUMO reach: 47-51% of HCPs; Rivfloza reach: lower across all segments
On-list HCPs have higher reach for both products; off-list HCPs have lower reach

Message Performance — OXLUMO:

OX1 ("only approved quarterly SC injection for PH1, all ages, all kidney stages"): Most recalled (66%) AND most effective (ME 70%)
OX5 ("ILLUMINATE A and B: eGFR stable at 30-36 months"): High recall (62%), good effectiveness (ME 65%)
OX2 ("tested in severely affected, advanced kidney disease, dialysis patients"): Low recall (38%) but HIGH effectiveness (ME 69%) — opportunity to amplify
OX3 ("largest PH1 trial program"): Low recall (38%) but high effectiveness (ME 66%) — another underleveraged message
OX8 ("Alnylam Assist, 94% pay nothing, 95% payer coverage"): Patient support message with solid recall
Average OXLUMO message recall: 50%; average ME: 65% — both above rare disease industry averages

Message Performance — Rivfloza:

RI2 ("approved for PH1 age ≥2, now prescribable"): Most recalled
RI9 ("only self/caregiver-administered PH1 treatment"): Most effective message across BOTH brands (ME up to 90% in interim data)
RI3 ("significantly reduced UOx in most PH1 patients — PHYOX2"): High effectiveness
Overall: OXLUMO messages rated MORE effective on average than Rivfloza messages; however, Rivfloza's self-administration message (RI9) is the single most effective message across brands

Sales Rep Effectiveness:

OXLUMO reps rate at parity or better than Rivfloza reps across all rep attributes
OXLUMO reps more likely to get "recommend to colleagues" and "compelling reason to Rx"
Rivfloza reps rate higher in favorably changing product perception — driven by novelty
Key OXLUMO opportunity: Rep preparation/organization (slightly below industry average — correlated with overall call quality)

Longitudinal Trends (Q4 2023 Baseline → Q3 2025):

OXLUMO reach declined from 81% (Q4 2023) to 47% (Q3 2025) — significant drop; Rivfloza entry split the share of voice
OXLUMO overall sales call quality remained high: 79% (Q4 2023) → 83% (Q3 2025)
Likelihood to increase Rx improved: 59% (Q4 2023) → 66% (Q3 2025)
Message recall average stable: 44% (Q4 2023) → 50% (Q3 2025) — improvement
Net Promoter Score improved from 10 (Q4 2023) to 88% recommend to colleagues (Q3 2025)
Rivfloza interim data shows reps catching up on key metrics — Rivfloza reps more likely to positively impact likelihood to prescribe and product perception

3.4 MSL and Non-Personal Promotion

MSL impact is similar for OXLUMO and Rivfloza; HCPs agree MSLs impacted understanding of appropriate product use more than clinical understanding of PH1
Over half of HCPs received emails from Alnylam sales reps in past 90 days (directionally greater than Novo Nordisk)
Efficacy is the most aided/unaided recalled topic for both manufacturers' digital communications
Preferred information sources: online medical journals/magazines (#1), emails from sales reps (#2)
Community HCPs rely more on sales rep interactions; academic HCPs rely more on journals/publications


4. Market Access and Pricing
4.1 OXLUMO Pricing and Access

OXLUMO WAC (wholesale acquisition cost): ~$493,000/year (approximate; weight-based dosing varies)
Ultra-rare orphan drug pricing model
Alnylam Assist patient support program:

94% of OXLUMO patients have $0 out-of-pocket costs
95% of payers offer national coverage
Commercial copay program for commercially insured patients
Patient Assistance Program (PAP) for uninsured/underinsured
Dedicated case managers assigned within 2 business days


Value-based agreement framework with Patient Need Adjustment (PNA) component — provides payers predictability on per-patient costs
High OOP costs and insurance coverage remain the #1 prescribing barrier — even among OXLUMO users; barrier is MORE pronounced among non-users and urologists

4.2 Market Access Dynamics

Insurance coverage issues and high OOP costs cited as top barrier by both nephrologists and urologists
Non-users perceive OOP costs as a greater barrier than users — suggesting once access is secured, perception improves
Rivfloza benefits from a perception of better affordability/access even among HCPs without direct experience — Alnylam has an opportunity to counter this with real-world access data
Access/formulary considerations are a more prominent prescribing driver for Rivfloza than OXLUMO
Formulary access/reimbursement sheets used more often in Rivfloza high-impact interactions — suggesting Novo Nordisk is leaning into the access narrative


5. Key Implications for ZoomRx Engagement
5.1 Study Portfolio
ZoomRx currently runs two primary study types for OXLUMO:


Promotional Effectiveness Tracker (PET):

Tracks OXLUMO vs Rivfloza rep interactions, messaging, sales rep effectiveness
Ongoing since Q4 2023 (baseline wave); latest wave Q3 2025
Head-to-head: HCPs can report interactions for either or both products
30 unique physicians per wave; Nephrology + Urology
Industry average benchmarked against rare disease indications



Awareness, Trial, and Usage (ATU):

Full market landscape study: awareness, treatment patterns, switching behavior, product perceptions, barriers
Wave 1 fielded Nov-Dec 2024; 60 HCPs
Covers hypothetical patient profiles to understand treatment decision-making
Includes rep interaction recall and information source preferences

Alnylam Pharmaceuticals: Pioneer of RNAi therapeutics; first FDA-approved RNAi drug (ONPATTRO, 2018)
Rare disease portfolio: ONPATTRO/AMVUTTRA (hATTR), GIVLAARI (AHP), OXLUMO (PH1)
Combined 2026 guidance: $5,300M net product revenue (~71% growth vs 2025)
"Alnylam 2030" strategy launched January 2026 — positions next era of growth
Same Alnylam rep may detail OXLUMO and GIVLAARI (combined PET readout covers both)
